figures.gloss: The itinerant Yoruba praying-man (c. 1879–1933), called Baba Aladura, who was summoned in 1925 to pray over the teenaged Christianah Abiodun Akinsowon after her days-long trance vision; from their partnership grew the Cherubim & Seraphim, the white-robed visionary society that spread across Yorubaland within a decade.
figures.facet: A founding that takes two: the movement begins not with one prophet but with the pairing of an elder intercessor and a young female visionary — and the C&S has carried a strong female prophetic stream ever since.
figures.provenance: J.D.Y. Peel, 'Aladura: A Religious Movement among the Yoruba' (1968); C&S founding accounts of the 1925 Corpus Christi vision.

figures.gloss: The road-gang steamroller driver (1904–1959) who heard a call in 1928, and whose July 1930 revival at Oke-Ooye, Ilesa — with reports of the dead raised and thousands healed by sanctified water — detonated the Aladura movement into a mass phenomenon; his stream became the Christ Apostolic Church after splitting with its British Pentecostal partners over the use of medicine.
figures.facet: The revival that chose water over quinine: Babalola's churches broke with mission Christianity precisely on the question of whether God heals without medicine — divine healing as the non-negotiable center, not an accessory.
figures.provenance: Peel (1968) on the 1930 revival and the Christ Apostolic Church split; H.W. Turner, 'History of an African Independent Church' (1967) for the wider Aladura context.

figures.gloss: The Porto-Novo carpenter (1909–1985) whose visionary call — tied in Celestial Church accounts to the May 1947 solar eclipse that caught him alone in the forest — founded the Celestial Church of Christ in 1947: barefoot worship, white sutana robes, ranked orders of prophets and visioners, and a liturgy dense with candles, incense, and revealed detail.
figures.facet: The most liturgically elaborated Aladura church: where C&S grew from a vision and CAC from a revival, the CCC grew from a continuous stream of trance-revelation that specifies everything from robe colors to ritual numbers.
figures.provenance: Afe Adogame, 'Celestial Church of Christ: The Politics of Cultural Identity in a West African Prophetic-Charismatic Movement' (1999).

figures.gloss: Water sanctified by prayer — the central Aladura sacrament of healing: drunk, bathed in, sprinkled on houses, dispensed at revivals by the barrel. At Ilesa in 1930 it was Babalola's stream-water that carried the revival's power; in the praying churches it remains the standing answer to sickness, barrenness, and attack.
figures.facet: Peel's key observation embodied: holy water answers the same pragmatic demand — protection, healing, children — that Yoruba religion had always serviced, but re-sourced entirely to the Christian God through prayer.
figures.provenance: Peel (1968) on prayer-sanctified water as functional successor to the herbalist's agbo infusion.

figures.gloss: Dream and waking vision as the living channel of revelation: Aladura churches maintain rosters of prophets and 'visioners' whose seen messages — warnings, diagnoses, callings, robe-colors, names — are delivered to individuals and congregations as routine spiritual intelligence.
figures.facet: Revelation as an ongoing institution rather than a closed canon: the praying churches bureaucratized the vision, with recognized offices, testing procedures, and recorded messages — ecstasy given a filing system.
figures.provenance: Peel (1968); Turner (1967) on the visionary offices of the Church of the Lord (Aladura).

figures.gloss: The Aladura world remains a contested field: witches, evil spirits, and human 'enemies' are real agents of sickness and misfortune, and prevailing prayer, fasting, psalms, and holy water are the weapons against them. The orisha cult was dropped; the Yoruba diagnosis of affliction was kept.
figures.facet: The substrate showing through the seam: Aladura Christianity rejected Yoruba gods but retained the Yoruba enemy — the movement's power lies in taking witchcraft fear seriously where mission churches dismissed it.
figures.provenance: Peel (1968) on the continuity of Yoruba assumptions about spiritual causation within Aladura prayer.

edges.note: The 1930 Ilesa revival ran on Babalola's sanctified water — prophet and sacrament are one event.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): the 19th-century Yoruba Bible rendered Satan as 'Èṣù,' and Aladura demonology inherited the equation — the orisha of the crossroads recast as the Enemy the praying churches battle.

GOLD SEAM (kin wave): the Ijebu-Ode prayer band affiliated with Philadelphia's Faith Tabernacle from c. 1920, and Babalola's revival churches partnered with the British Apostolic Church from 1931 — the healing doctrine arrived through documented correspondence and mission, then was made Nigerian.

Both movements re-open revelation in the present tense — the Spirit speaking now, through ordinary believers — but Aladura channels it through seen visions and dreams where classical Pentecostalism privileges tongues.

Parallel founding prophets of the two great African-initiated church families of the same generation — both streams seeded in part by American divine-healing networks (Faith Tabernacle in Nigeria, Dowie's Zion in South Africa), both re-rooting Christianity in African healing pragmatics.

dossier.essence: The Aladura ('owners of prayer,' from Yoruba àdúrà) are the family of praying churches that rose among the Yoruba of western Nigeria from 1918, when an influenza-epidemic prayer band at Ijebu-Ode turned from mission medicine to divine healing. Its major streams — the Cherubim & Seraphim (1925), the Church of the Lord (Aladura) (1930), the Christ Apostolic Church grown from Joseph Ayo Babalola's 1930 Ilesa revival, and the Celestial Church of Christ (1947) — now count millions of members in Nigeria and a worldwide diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is the biblical one laid directly over the Yoruba one: a single sovereign God whose power answers prevailing prayer, and a contested middle world where witches, evil spirits, and human enemies (ọ̀tá) remain real causes of sickness, barrenness, and misfortune. The orisha were renounced as idolatry, but the Yoruba diagnosis of affliction was kept; revelation stays open through dream and vision, and death leads to a broadly Christian judgment and afterlife.
dossier.practice: Prayer is the technology: fasting, psalms, and water sanctified by blessing (omi iye) replace both traditional medicine-making and, in the Christ Apostolic stream, Western medicine itself. White robes, barefoot worship, and ranked orders of prophets and visioners mark the churches; the visionary consultation — diagnosis and prescription delivered from dream or trance — is the everyday front door, and Celestial Church liturgy elaborates revealed detail down to robe colors and ritual numbers.
dossier.canon: The Bible — in Yoruba and English — is the sole scripture, supplemented in practice by an unclosed, institutionally managed stream of recorded visions, dreams, and prophetic messages.
dossier.reflects: Revelation kept deliberately open and then bureaucratized — offices, testing procedures, and rosters for the vision — the impulse to institutionalize ecstasy without closing the channel.
sources: J.D.Y. Peel, 'Aladura: A Religious Movement among the Yoruba' (1968)
H.W. Turner, 'History of an African Independent Church: The Church of the Lord (Aladura)' (1967)
Afe Adogame, 'Celestial Church of Christ: The Politics of Cultural Identity in a West African Prophetic-Charismatic Movement' (1999)
